title: Constants, Literals, Domain Types, and Configuration · Repository Factory
summary: Replace ambiguous magic values without creating meaningless constant noise. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a domain constant and configuration policy while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish harmless local literals from repeated domain rules, units, limits, and protocol values.
Name stable domain values by meaning and keep them near their owning policy.
Use enums, unions, literal types, branded values, or value objects for closed domains.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# Risk-profiled repository gate portfolio
# Each gate needs purpose, command, failure fixture, owner, and suppression policy.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
Use a changed workshop case derived from this module: gates constants literals. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.

content.text: Distinguish harmless local literals from repeated domain rules, units, limits, and protocol values. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Check preconditions, perform the operation in an inspectable order, then verify the result and a boundary case.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Use a changed worked example case derived from this module: gates constants literals. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result. A defensible solution applies Literal Risk, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a domain constant and configuration policy.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Every numeric or string literal must become a constant. This fails the Literal Risk contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact
steps.instruction: Append a gates-constant-tests-brniu: manifest entry with concrete verify plus failure, owner, or repair evidence.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Constant Tests into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
gates-constant-tests-brniu:
  verify: [replace with a deterministic check]
  failure: [replace with the rejected condition]
  repair: [replace with a local reproduction and correction path]
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Constant Tests condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Constant Tests applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Test boundary values and downstream behavior instead of only constant equality.
